nav.featuresDesc: Every situation and form we cover

hero.badge: Free federal filing for simple returns
hero.heading: File your taxes in minutes — not hours
hero.subheading: Taxly guides you step by step through your federal and state return in plain English. No jargon. No surprise fees. Maximum refund.
hero.noCard: No credit card required · Free federal return

howItWorks.heading: Three steps. You stay in control.
howItWorks.subheading: We do the math. You make the decisions.

step1.title: Import your docs
step1.description: Upload your W-2, 1099s, or connect directly to your employer. Taxly pulls in your numbers automatically.
details: Upload W-2 and 1099 PDFs, or connect your employer directly
Bring last year’s return as a PDF from TurboTax or H&R Block
Missing a document? Skip it and come back any time before you file

step2.title: Answer plain questions
step2.description: No tax forms, no jargon. We ask simple questions and put your answers in the right boxes behind the scenes.
details: Plain-English questions — never a raw IRS form field
Your answers fill the right boxes on the 1040 behind the scenes
The price you see at the start is locked — more questions never raise it

step3.title: Review & e-file
step3.description: See your refund or balance due before you file. E-file in one click — the IRS typically processes in 21 days.
details: See the full return and your refund or balance due before anything is sent
E-file federal and state in one click
The IRS confirms receipt — refunds typically process within 21 days

whatYouNeed.heading: What you’ll need
whatYouNeed.intro: Most filers finish with just the first column. The rest only applies if it applies to you.

groups.title: Everyone
items: Social Security numbers for you, your spouse, and dependents
Bank account and routing numbers for direct deposit
Last year’s tax return (optional — it speeds things up)

groups.title: Self-employed or freelance
items: A summary of business income and expenses
Receipts for deductions you plan to claim

whatYouNeed.tip: Don’t hunt everything down up front — the interview asks for each document only when your answers make it relevant, and you can save and come back.

imports.heading: Import from anywhere
imports.description: Connect your employer, brokerage, or bank. Or upload a PDF from TurboTax, H&R Block, or last year's Taxly return.

situations.heading: It covers your situation
situations.subheading: Whether you're a W-2 employee or running a side hustle, Taxly has you covered.

employee.title: W-2 Employee
employee.description: Simple filing for salaried or hourly workers. File federal free.

freelancer.title: Freelancer & Gig Worker
freelancer.description: 1099-NEC, Schedule C, self-employment tax, quarterly estimates — all handled.

investor.title: Investor
investor.description: Stocks, crypto, dividends, capital gains. Import from your brokerage automatically.

homeowner.title: Homeowner
homeowner.description: Mortgage interest, property tax, home-office deduction — we find every dollar.

family.title: Family & Dependents
family.description: Child Tax Credit, Child & Dependent Care, education credits, and more.

retirement.title: Retirement Income
retirement.description: Social Security, 401(k), IRA distributions, pension income — filed correctly.

security.heading: Your tax data is safe with us
security.subheading: We handle your most sensitive financial information. Here's exactly how we protect it.

encryption.title: AES-256 Encryption at Rest
encryption.description: Every piece of data you enter — your SSN, income, deductions — is encrypted with AES-256 before it ever touches our database. The same standard used by banks and the US government.

transit.title: TLS 1.3 in Transit
transit.description: All data moving between your browser and our servers is protected by TLS 1.3. Your information is never sent in plain text, ever.

mfa.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
mfa.description: Protect your account with a second factor — authenticator app or SMS. We recommend enabling MFA before you start filing.

noSell.title: We Never Sell Your Data
noSell.description: Your tax information is yours. We don't sell it, share it with advertisers, or use it to build profiles. We use it for one thing: filing your taxes.

retention.title: Data Retention & Deletion
retention.description: We retain your filed returns for 7 years so you can access them anytime. You can request full deletion of your account and data at any time.

access.title: Strict Access Controls
access.description: Taxly employees cannot access your tax data without your explicit permission. All internal access is logged and audited.

security.certNote: We are actively pursuing SOC 2 Type II certification. Our infrastructure follows its controls today.
security.chip: How your data will be protected
security.preLaunchNote: Honest status: Taxly opens for TY2026 returns in January 2027, and this page describes the security build it launches with. Today this website stores almost nothing — the note below has the details.

today.heading: And today, before launch?
today.text: This marketing site has no accounts and no tax-data database. The free calculators run entirely in your browser, and the only things we keep are a waitlist email if you give one and a contact message if you send one. The security described above ships with the product — and will be re-verified before any taxpayer data flows.

guaranteesPage.heading: Three promises we actually keep
guaranteesPage.subheading: No fine print. No asterisks. Here's exactly what each guarantee means and what you get.

maxRefund.title: Maximum Refund Guarantee
maxRefund.whatText: If you file the same return with another major tax software (TurboTax, H&R Block, FreeTaxUSA) and they produce a larger federal refund using identical data, we'll refund your Taxly filing fee in full.
maxRefund.howText: File with Taxly first. Then file with the other software. If their refund is higher, email us at hello@gettaxly.com within 60 days of your Taxly e-file date with both return summaries. We'll verify and refund within 5 business days.
maxRefund.limits: This guarantee covers federal returns only and requires that the same deductions and income are entered in both returns.

accuracy.title: 100% Accuracy Guarantee
accuracy.whatText: If the IRS or a state tax authority assesses a penalty or interest charge that results directly from a Taxly calculation error — not from information you entered incorrectly — we'll pay that penalty and interest.
accuracy.howText: Contact us at hello@gettaxly.com with your IRS notice. We'll review whether the error originated in Taxly's calculations. If it did, we cover the penalty and interest charges. No arguments, no delays.
accuracy.limits: This does not cover penalties from incorrect information you entered, late filing, or situations where you chose to override Taxly's recommendations.

audit.title: Audit Support
audit.whatText: If the IRS or a state tax authority contacts you about a return you filed with Taxly, we'll provide guidance at every step of your response — what the notice means, what documents to gather, and what to say.
audit.howText: Forward the IRS or state notice to support@gettaxly.com. A tax specialist will review it and get back to you within one business day with a plain-English explanation and next steps.
audit.limits: Audit support means guidance and assistance, not legal representation. For full representation before the IRS, consult a licensed CPA or tax attorney.

guaranteesPage.preLaunchNote: Honest status: Taxly opens for TY2026 returns in January 2027. These are the guarantees we launch with — the full written policy terms will be published on this page before we accept a single return.
guaranteesPage.chip: In writing before we accept a return

cta.note: Hold us to all three. Join the waitlist and file when Taxly opens for TY2026.

maxRefund.title: Maximum Refund Guarantee
maxRefund.description: If another tax software gets you a larger refund using the same data, we'll refund your filing fee.

accuracy.title: 100% Accuracy Guarantee
accuracy.description: If Taxly makes a calculation error that costs you a penalty, we pay the penalty plus interest.

audit.title: Audit Support
audit.description: If the IRS contacts you about a return filed with Taxly, we'll walk you through every step of the response.

pricing.heading: One honest price. No surprises at checkout.
pricing.subheading: The number you see is the number you pay.

free.name: Free
free.description: Simple W-2 returns. No income limits.

q1.question: Is the Free tier really free — no hidden fees?
q1.answer: Yes. Free means $0 for federal and $0 for one state return. We don't ask for your credit card until you upgrade to Deluxe or Premium. The price you see when you start is the price you pay.

q2.question: How are state returns priced?
q2.answer: Every plan includes your first state return — Free, Deluxe, and Premium. Additional states are $29 each: a second state adds $29, a third adds $58. State pricing is shown before you start and locked like everything else.

q3.question: Can I upgrade after I start?
q3.answer: Yes. Start on Free and upgrade to Deluxe or Premium any time before you file. Your data carries over and you only pay when you e-file.

q4.question: Do prices change next year?
q4.answer: We lock your price for the tax year you file. If you start your 2026 return at $39, that's what you pay — even if we raise prices in 2027.

q5.question: Is there a refund policy?
q5.answer: If you paid and have not yet e-filed, contact us within 30 days and we'll refund you. Once a return is e-filed and accepted by the IRS, we cannot refund the filing fee.

honestMaths.heading: The honest maths: three ways to file
honestMaths.intro: Including the two that don't involve paying us. If one of these fits, use it — sending you to software you don't need is the exact behaviour this product exists to reject.

directFile.title: IRS Direct File
directFile.text: Free and official. If your income is all W-2, you take the standard deduction, and your state participates — this is simply the right choice.

freetaxusa.title: FreeTaxUSA
freetaxusa.text: The honest budget option — genuinely. If you know your tax situation and a form-like interface doesn't slow you down, it's the cheapest credible way to file.

taxly.title: Taxly Premium
taxly.text: What the premium buys: a plain-English guided interview that finds deductions a first-time 1099 filer doesn't know exist — at a price that can't change at checkout.

pricing.chip: Flat · disclosed · locked when you start
trust: The price you see when you start is the price you pay
Every plan includes your first state return

waitlist.heading: Taxly opens for TY2026 in January 2027
waitlist.note: Join the waitlist for first access. Simple returns free, Deluxe $39, Premium $69 — flat, disclosed up front, locked when you start.

featuresPage.heading: Every situation, covered
featuresPage.subheading: Taxly supports the tax situations that matter most for US individuals and families.

help.heading: Help Center
help.subheading: Quick answers to the most common questions.

q1.question: Do I need to create an account to start?
q1.answer: Yes, a free account is required to save your progress and file your return. It takes under a minute to sign up — just an email and password.

q2.question: Which tax year can I file with Taxly?
q2.answer: You can file the current tax year return (due April 15) and amend returns from the prior two years.

q3.question: Can I import my return from another software?
q3.answer: Yes. Upload a PDF of your prior-year TurboTax, H&R Block, or Taxly return and we'll pre-fill your basic info and prior-year figures.

q4.question: Can I use Taxly on mobile?
q4.answer: Yes. Taxly is fully responsive and works on any smartphone or tablet browser. A dedicated mobile app is planned.

q1.question: When do I pay?
q1.answer: You only pay when you e-file your return. You can prepare your return and see your refund estimate completely free before deciding.

q2.question: What payment methods do you accept?
q2.answer: We accept all major credit and debit cards (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover) and Apple Pay / Google Pay.

q3.question: Can I get a refund?
q3.answer: If you've paid but not yet e-filed, contact us within 30 days for a full refund. After e-filing, the fee is non-refundable.

q1.question: Is my Social Security number safe?
q1.answer: Yes. Your SSN is encrypted with AES-256 before storage and is never transmitted in plain text. It's only sent to the IRS during e-filing, over an encrypted connection.

q2.question: Does Taxly sell my data?
q2.answer: Never. We use your data solely to prepare and file your taxes. We do not sell it, share it with advertisers, or use it to build advertising profiles.

q3.question: How do I enable two-factor authentication?
q3.answer: Go to Account Settings → Security → Enable 2FA. We support authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy) and SMS.

q1.question: How long does IRS processing take?
q1.answer: The IRS typically accepts e-filed returns within 24–48 hours and issues refunds within 21 days of acceptance. You can check status at IRS.gov/refunds.

q2.question: What if I make a mistake after filing?
q2.answer: You can amend your return using Form 1040-X. Taxly will guide you through the amendment process. There's no additional filing fee for amendments.

q3.question: How do I file an extension?
q3.answer: File Form 4868 by April 15 to get a 6-month extension to October 15. Taxly handles this automatically. Note: an extension to file is not an extension to pay — any tax owed is still due April 15.

q4.question: Can I file jointly with my spouse?
q4.answer: Yes. Choose 'Married Filing Jointly' when you start. Taxly will ask for your spouse's information and compare MFJ vs MFS to find the better outcome.

help.preLaunchNote: Honest status: Taxly opens for TY2026 returns in January 2027. These answers describe the product we're building — anything operational (accounts, payments, refunds, 2FA) applies from launch.

q1.question: Can I file my taxes with Taxly today?
q1.answer: Not yet. Taxly opens for TY2026 returns in January 2027. Until then, this site is the honest preview: what the product does, what it costs, and how it treats your data — plus free calculators and guides that work today.

q2.question: What can I actually use right now?
q2.answer: Three free calculators (refund, IRS penalties, quarterly estimated tax), the plain-English guides, and the tax glossary. None of them ask for a signup, and the numbers you type never leave your browser.

q3.question: What does joining the waitlist commit me to?
q3.answer: Nothing. It's permission to email you about the launch — no card, no fee, and unsubscribe is one click in every email. A spot on the list simply means you hear first when doors open.

toolsBand.heading: Need an answer with a number in it?
toolsBand.text: The free tools work today, waitlist or not — and they answer the three questions people bring to a help page most often: what's my refund, what does being late cost, and what should I be paying each quarter.

contact.heading: Still stuck?
contact.text: Send us a message and a human reads it. Pre-launch we're a small team, so the honest promise is a thoughtful reply, not an instant one.

about.heading: Built by people who believe taxes shouldn't be this hard
about.subheading: Taxly is made by the team behind Finovo — accounting software for European small businesses. We saw how broken the US tax filing experience was and decided to fix it.

mission.heading: Our mission
mission.text:
The average American spends 13 hours filing their taxes. That's a full workday lost to paperwork, jargon, and anxiety — every year. We think that's wrong.

Taxly exists to give those hours back. Guided questions instead of confusing forms. Flat prices instead of bait-and-switch fees. Plain English instead of IRS jargon.

We're not trying to replace your accountant. We're trying to make sure you don't need one for a simple return.

team.heading: Who builds Taxly
team.text:
We're AppsLab — a small, compliance-first software company. Finovo, our European accounting product, is used by thousands of freelancers and small businesses in the UK, France, and Germany.

Taxly brings the same engineering rigour and obsessive attention to accuracy to the US market.

contact.heading: Get in touch
contact.subheading: Questions, feedback, or press enquiries — we read everything.

form.name: Your name
form.subject: Subject
form.success: Message sent — a human will read it. Pre-launch we are a small team, so expect a thoughtful reply rather than an instant one.
form.error: Something went wrong. Please try again or email us directly.
form.noPii: Please don’t include your SSN or other sensitive numbers — we don’t need them to answer you.

info.emailText: Skip the form entirely — the same inbox, the same humans.
info.expectText: We read everything. Pre-launch we are a small team, so the honest promise is a thoughtful reply, not an instant one — and no autoresponder pretending otherwise.
info.beforeText: We can’t prepare or review your return yet — Taxly opens in January 2027. For tax questions today, these are the fastest paths:

privacy.heading: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: The short version: today this site collects almost nothing — a waitlist email if you give it, a message if you send one, and analytics only if you say yes. Here is the whole picture, in plain English.

sections.heading: Who we are and what this covers
intro: Taxly (“we”, “us”) operates gettaxly.com. Today the site is a pre-launch marketing site: it explains the product we are building, offers free calculators, and runs a waitlist. This policy describes what the website actually collects today — which is deliberately little.

The Taxly filing product is not yet live. Before it collects a single piece of taxpayer data, we will publish an updated policy covering it (see “When the filing product launches” below) — and those practices will be legal commitments, not marketing.

sections.heading: What we collect today
intro: The site has no user accounts and no database of visitors. What we process:
bullets: Waitlist: the email address you give us — nothing else.
Contact form: your name, email address, and message, delivered to us as an email.
Cookie choice: whether you accepted or declined analytics, stored in your browser.
Analytics (only if you accept): pages visited, approximate location derived from IP, and device/browser type — via Google Analytics 4 and Microsoft Clarity.
Hosting logs: standard request metadata (IP address, user agent) processed by our host to serve and secure the site.
outro: We do not collect Social Security numbers, income details, or any tax information through this website. The free calculators run in your browser — the numbers you type are not sent to or stored on our servers.

sections.heading: How we use it
bullets: Your waitlist email — to send the launch updates you asked for.
Your contact details — to reply to you.
Consent-gated analytics — to understand, in aggregate, which content helps.
Hosting logs — to keep the site up and abuse out.
outro: We do not use any of it for advertising, we do not build profiles, and we never sell personal information.

sections.heading: Sharing
intro: Personal information goes only to the service providers that run the site for us — listed by name on our Sub-processors page — and to no one else. We do not sell personal information, and we do not share it for cross-context behavioral advertising.
We would disclose information if a law required it; if that ever happens we will say so here unless we are legally barred from doing so.

sections.heading: Cookies and analytics
intro: Analytics load only after you explicitly accept — declining is a first-class choice and the site works identically either way. The full list of cookies, their durations, and a control to change your choice live on the Cookie Policy page.

sections.heading: Retention
bullets: Waitlist email: until launch or until you unsubscribe — one click, in every email.
Contact messages: for as long as the conversation needs, then deleted.
Analytics data: for the durations listed in the Cookie Policy.
Hosting logs: for the short operational windows our host keeps them.

sections.heading: Your rights and choices
intro: You can ask what we hold about you, have it corrected, or have it deleted. We honor these requests from everyone — not only where a statute such as the CCPA requires it — and we do not treat you differently for asking.
To exercise any of them, use the contact page. Removing a waitlist email is also one click via the unsubscribe link.

sections.heading: Children
intro: The site is not directed at children under 16 and we do not knowingly collect their information.

sections.heading: When the filing product launches
intro: The product will handle the most sensitive category of consumer data — Social Security numbers, income, dependents, bank details. That phase is governed by specific law, and these are the commitments the product is being built to:
bullets: A written information security program per the FTC GLBA Safeguards Rule and IRS Publication 4557.
Column-level encryption for SSN and bank fields, mandatory multi-factor authentication, and append-only audit logging.
Your return shared only with the IRS and the state you file in, only to file it, and only as you authorize — with consent flows per IRS Publication 1345.
No advertising use of tax data. Ever.
outro: None of this is in effect today, because none of that data is collected today. This policy will be re-issued, re-dated, and put in front of you before any of it begins.

sections.heading: Changes to this policy
intro: When this policy changes we update the date at the top. If a change is material, we tell the waitlist by email before it takes effect.

sections.heading: Contact
intro: Privacy questions: use the contact page and we will answer by email.

terms.heading: Terms of Service
terms.intro: The short version: the site is free, informational, and sells nothing yet. Here are the rules for using it, in plain English.

sections.heading: What these terms cover
intro: These terms govern your use of gettaxly.com. Today that means the guides and articles, the free calculators, and the waitlist. By using the site you agree to them.

The Taxly filing product is not yet available. Product terms — e-file consents, fees, guarantees — take effect only at launch; a preview is in “At launch” below, and you will accept the final version before the product ever handles your return.

sections.heading: Software and content, not advice
intro: Taxly is software and publishing — not a CPA firm, a law firm, or a licensed tax preparer. Guides, comparisons, and tools are general information for US filers; they are not tax, legal, or financial advice for your specific situation. For advice on your facts, talk to a qualified professional.

sections.heading: The free calculators
bullets: They produce estimates from exactly what you type, using simplified models of federal rules.
They run in your browser — your inputs are not stored on our servers.
Figures can drift between annual IRS updates; each tool links the primary source to verify against.
They are free and require no signup.
outro: Do not make final filing decisions on a calculator result alone — verify against the primary source, or against the product once it exists.

sections.heading: The waitlist
intro: Joining costs nothing and commits you to nothing — it is permission to email you about the launch, and unsubscribe is one click. A place on the waitlist is not a guarantee of availability, of coverage for your state, or of any price other than the ones we publish.

sections.heading: Acceptable use
bullets: Don’t probe, disable, or interfere with the site’s security or other people’s use of it.
Don’t scrape at abusive volume.
Don’t misrepresent an affiliation with Taxly.
Don’t use the site for anything unlawful.

sections.heading: Intellectual property
intro: The site — its copy, design, tools, and the Taxly name and mark — belongs to us. Use it personally and non-commercially; don’t republish it as your own.

sections.heading: Third-party links
intro: We link to primary sources (irs.gov, state revenue departments) and to competitors where honesty requires it. We don’t control those sites and aren’t responsible for their content or practices.

sections.heading: At launch (a preview — not yet in force)
bullets: Pay-at-file: you pay only when you file, and the price shown when you start is the price at checkout — the price-lock promise.
E-file consents per IRS Publication 1345, collected in the product.
Guarantees (accuracy, maximum refund) will exist only as written, funded policies published on the guarantees page.
Updated Terms presented to you before the product takes a dollar or a return.
outro: Until then, nothing on this site sells you anything — there are no fees today.

sections.heading: Disclaimers
intro: The site is provided “as is” and “as available”, without warranties of any kind, express or implied — including the accuracy of calculator outputs and uninterrupted availability.

sections.heading: Limitation of liability
intro: To the maximum extent the law allows, we are not liable for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages arising from your use of this free site, and our total liability is limited to the greater of $50 or the amount you have paid us (today: nothing). Some jurisdictions don’t allow these limits, so parts of this may not apply to you.

sections.heading: Governing law
intro: These terms are governed by the laws of the United States. The governing state and venue will be fixed with counsel before launch — this draft flags the open item rather than inventing one.

sections.heading: Changes to these terms
intro: We may update these terms; the date above moves when we do. Material changes are announced to the waitlist by email at least 14 days before they take effect.

sections.heading: Contact
intro: Questions about these terms: use the contact page.

q1.question: Is Taxly really free for simple returns?
q1.answer: Yes. Federal filing is free for W-2 income with the standard deduction, no income limit. One state return is also free.

q2.question: When is the tax deadline?
q2.answer: The federal deadline is April 15 for most filers. If you need more time, you can file a free extension to October 15 — Taxly handles that too.

q3.question: Is my data secure?
q3.answer: All data is encrypted with AES-256 at rest and TLS 1.3 in transit. We never sell your data or use it for advertising.

q4.question: Can I import from last year's return?
q4.answer: Yes. Import a PDF from TurboTax, H&R Block, or any prior Taxly return and we'll pre-fill your name, address, and prior-year figures.

q5.question: What if I have both W-2 and freelance income?
q5.answer: Start with Premium. It handles W-2 plus Schedule C (self-employment) in the same return, including self-employment tax calculation.

q6.question: How fast is my refund?
q6.answer: The IRS typically deposits refunds within 21 days of acceptance for e-filed returns. State refunds vary by state.

refundEstimator.heading: Estimate your federal tax refund
refundEstimator.subheading: Answer 5 questions. See your estimate instantly. No account needed.

result.enterIncome: Enter your income to see your estimate
result.estimate: Estimate only — based on projected TY2026 figures

cta.heading: Ready to file your actual return?
cta.sub: Taxly walks you through the same questions — and does the filing too.

refundEstimator.disclaimer: This tool provides estimates only and does not constitute tax advice. Based on projected TY2026 tax law. Actual results may differ. Consult a tax professional for your specific situation.

faq.q: How accurate is this refund estimate?
faq.a: It's a directional estimate built from the numbers you enter and stated assumptions. Your actual refund depends on your complete return — every credit, deduction, and the exact withholding on your W-2.

faq.q: Do I need an account, and do you store what I type?
faq.a: No. The estimator runs in your browser, needs no signup and no email, and we don't store what you enter.

faq.q: Why might my actual refund differ from the estimate?
faq.a: Common reasons: extra income the estimate didn't include (interest, gig work), credits that phase in or out with income, and state taxes — this tool focuses on your federal picture.

faq.q: When will I get my refund after filing?
faq.a: The IRS typically issues refunds within 21 days of accepting an e-filed return. You can track yours at irs.gov/refunds.

cookiesPage.heading: Cookie Policy
cookiesPage.intro: This page lists every cookie and similar technology gettaxly.com uses, what it does, and how long it lasts. Analytics cookies load only after you accept them in the consent banner — declining keeps the site fully functional.
cookiesPage.consentNote: Your consent choice itself is stored in your browser's localStorage (not a cookie) under the key gf_cookie_consent, so we can remember your decision without tracking you.

rows.name: gf_cookie_consent
rows.purpose: Remembers whether you accepted or declined analytics

rows.name: _ga / _ga_*
rows.purpose: Distinguishes visitors and sessions for aggregate usage statistics

rows.name: _clck / _clsk
rows.purpose: Session replay and heatmaps to improve the site

rows.name: Vercel Analytics
rows.purpose: Cookieless, aggregated page-view counts (no identifier stored on your device)

cookiesPage.managing: You can withdraw consent at any time by clearing your browser data for this site — the banner will ask again on your next visit. Blocking all cookies in your browser also works; nothing on this site breaks without them.
cookiesPage.choicePending: No choice saved — the consent banner is showing
cookiesPage.changeNote: Clears your saved choice and shows the consent banner again.

subProcessorsPage.heading: Sub-processors
subProcessorsPage.intro: These are the third-party services that process data on our behalf when you use gettaxly.com. We list them by name because you can't judge our privacy promises without knowing who we share data with.

hub.heading: Free tax tools — no signup, no catch
hub.intro: Three calculators that answer the questions people actually search for. Every result is an illustrative estimate with the assumptions stated, and every rate links to the IRS source so you can verify it.

refund.title: Refund estimator
refund.text: A rough TY2026 federal refund or balance-due estimate from your income, withholding, and household.
needs: Your last pay stub or W-2

penalty.title: Penalty estimator
penalty.text: Missed the April deadline? Estimate failure-to-file and failure-to-pay penalties plus interest — and what to do right now.
needs: How many months past the deadline you are

quarterly.title: Quarterly tax calculator
quarterly.text: Freelance or gig income? Work out your safe-harbor quarterly payments and see the self-employment tax before it surprises you.

hub.note: These tools exist because good answers to scary questions are the most honest marketing we know.

which.heading: Not sure which one? Start from your situation

rows.situation: You just want to know whether April brings a refund or a bill.
The deadline passed, you owe tax, and you haven’t filed yet.
You filed on time but couldn’t pay the full balance.
You started freelancing and no employer withholds tax for you anymore.
You have a W-2 day job plus 1099 side income and want the whole picture.

examples.heading: Try one with example numbers
examples.intro: Not ready to dig out your own paperwork? Walk through one of these made-up filers first — type their numbers in, check you get the same answer, and see how the tool thinks before you trust it with your real ones.

items.title: Refund estimator
items.persona: Alex is single, works one W-2 job that paid $65,000 this year, and had $9,200 of federal tax withheld. No side income, no kids.
items.note: The breakdown shows why: Alex’s actual tax comes to $5,914, so the extra withholding comes back. A refund this size is an interest-free loan to the IRS — the fix is a W-4 adjustment, not a celebration.

items.title: Penalty estimator
items.persona: Sam owes $3,000, is two months past the April deadline, and — the expensive part — never filed the return.
steps: Leave “I filed my return on time” unchecked
items.resultLabel: Estimated total on top of the tax
items.note: Now tick the “I filed on time” box: the estimate drops to $65.00. That $270 gap is the whole lesson — filing costs nothing and removes the biggest penalty, even when you can’t pay a dollar yet.

items.title: Quarterly tax calculator
items.persona: Riley went freelance this year. Last year’s Form 1040 shows $12,000 of total tax, and a part-time W-2 job will withhold about $4,000 this year.
items.note: Four payments of $2,000 reach 100% of last year’s tax, so no underpayment penalty applies whatever this year’s bill turns out to be. The optional profit field also surfaces $5,651.82 of self-employment tax most first-year freelancers don’t see coming.

items.title: Everything runs in your browser
items.text: No signup, no name, no email, no SSN. The numbers you type never leave your device — there’s no server doing the math and nothing is stored.

items.title: Draft TY2026 figures, IRS-linked
items.text: Brackets, rates, and thresholds are based on current law; the IRS finalizes 2026 figures later. Every tool links its IRS source so you can verify the rates yourself.

items.title: Illustrative, not advice
items.text: Each result states its assumptions right next to the number. These are planning estimates — your return can differ, and edge cases like other credits, capital gains, or state tax aren’t modeled.

penalty.heading: IRS penalty estimator
penalty.intro: Missed the deadline with tax owed? Get an honest estimate of the failure-to-file penalty, the failure-to-pay penalty, and interest — and the moves that stop the bleeding.
penalty.fileFirstNote: The single most important fact: the penalty for not FILING is ten times the penalty for not PAYING. If you haven't filed, file now — even if you can't pay a dollar of it yet.
penalty.taxHint: The unpaid balance from your return (not your total tax).
penalty.monthsHint: Part of a month counts as a full month — that's the IRS rule, not ours.
penalty.filedLabel: I filed my return (or an extension) on time
penalty.filedHint: Filing on time removes the big 5%/month penalty; only late payment and interest remain.
penalty.errorBlank: Enter both amounts — a blank field isn't zero.
penalty.total: Estimated total on top of your tax
penalty.minimumNote: Your return is over 60 days late, so the minimum failure-to-file penalty applied — the lesser of the fixed floor or 100% of the unpaid tax.

advice.heading: What to do right now
advice.file: 1. File the return today if you haven't — that stops the 5%/month penalty immediately.
advice.plan: 2. Can't pay in full? The IRS offers payment plans; being on one cuts the failure-to-pay rate in half.
advice.abatement: 3. First time this happened? Ask the IRS about first-time penalty abatement — many filers qualify and never ask.
advice.cta: Taxly opens for TY2026 — join the waitlist

penalty.disclaimer: Illustrative estimate, not tax advice. Rates and the minimum-penalty floor change; verify current figures at irs.gov/payments/penalties before acting.

faq.q: How are IRS late-filing penalties calculated?
faq.a: The failure-to-file penalty is 5% of the unpaid tax per month (or part of a month), capped at 25%. The failure-to-pay penalty is 0.5% per month, also capped at 25%. In months where both apply, the combined charge is 5%. Interest accrues on top — verify current rates at irs.gov.

faq.q: Is there a minimum penalty for filing very late?
faq.a: Yes. If your return is more than 60 days late, a minimum penalty applies — a fixed dollar amount the IRS adjusts periodically, or 100% of the unpaid tax if that's smaller. Check the current figure on irs.gov.

faq.q: What if I'm owed a refund?
faq.a: Both penalties are percentages of unpaid tax, so if you're due a refund there is no failure-to-file or failure-to-pay penalty. But you must file within three years to claim the refund — after that it's forfeited.

faq.q: Is this estimate exact?
faq.a: No — it's an estimate for orientation. It doesn't include interest, first-time penalty abatement, or the reduced rate under a direct-debit payment plan. The IRS's own notice is the authoritative number.

quarterly.heading: Quarterly estimated tax calculator
quarterly.intro: No employer withholds for freelance income. This works out the safe-harbor payments that keep the IRS penalty away — from the one number you already know: last year's tax.
quarterly.priorHint: The “total tax” line on last year's Form 1040 — not your refund or payment.
quarterly.withholdingHint: If you also have a day job, its withholding counts toward the target. No job? Leave it at 0.
quarterly.highAgiHint: High earners must hit 110% of last year's tax instead of 100%.
quarterly.profitHint: Income minus expenses. Adding it shows the self-employment tax most first-year freelancers don't see coming.
quarterly.errorBlank: Enter last year's tax and your withholding — a blank field isn't zero.
quarterly.resultNote: Targets {pct} of last year's tax ({target}). Pay this through the year and there's no underpayment penalty, whatever this year's final bill turns out to be.
quarterly.dueDates: Due roughly April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15 — verify each year's exact dates at irs.gov.
quarterly.seHeading: Heads up: self-employment tax on that profit
quarterly.seNote: 15.3% Social Security and Medicare on 92.35% of profit — on top of income tax, because no employer is paying half of it for you. (Approximation: the Social Security portion caps at the annual wage base for high profits.)

cta.text: Taxly's Premium tier is built around exactly this return — 1099 income, deductions found, quarterly reminders.

quarterly.disclaimer: Illustrative estimate, not tax advice. Safe-harbor rules and dates come from IRS Form 1040-ES — verify the current year's figures at irs.gov.

faq.q: Who has to make quarterly estimated payments?
faq.a: Generally anyone who expects to owe at least $1,000 for the year after withholding and credits — typically freelancers, contractors, landlords, and investors. Employees with side income can often skip quarterlies by increasing paycheck withholding instead.

faq.q: What is the safe-harbor rule?
faq.a: Pay 100% of last year's total tax (110% if your adjusted gross income was over $150,000) in four installments and you avoid underpayment penalties no matter what you end up owing this year. Verify the details on irs.gov (Form 1040-ES).

faq.q: When are estimated payments due?
faq.a: Roughly April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15 of the following year — the periods are uneven, not true quarters. Dates shift for weekends and holidays; verify the current schedule on irs.gov.

faq.q: What happens if I miss a quarter?
faq.a: An underpayment penalty accrues per period, based on how long the shortfall stays unpaid. You can't retroactively fix a missed date, but paying as soon as you can stops the meter earlier and costs less.

tools.chipNote: No signup · runs in your browser

turbotaxAlternative.heading: Looking for a TurboTax alternative?
turbotaxAlternative.intro: Yes, we're a competitor — which is exactly why this guide names the free government options first, credits the budget rival that deserves it, and tells you plainly when you should just stay put. If a recommendation below isn't us, it's because it shouldn't be.

options.name: IRS Direct File
options.body: The IRS's own filing tool. Genuinely free — federal and participating-state returns — and genuinely good if your situation fits inside its lines: W-2 income, standard deduction, common credits. Availability moves with politics; check the current state list before planning on it.
options.linkLabel: How Taxly compares to Direct File

options.name: IRS Free File
options.body: Brand-name software free through an IRS partnership if your income is under the program's cap (it adjusts yearly — verify on irs.gov). The catch is the experience: you're routed to partner products whose free tier ends where your tax situation gets interesting.

options.name: FreeTaxUSA
options.body: The budget option that earns its reputation: free federal returns for most situations, with a modest per-state fee. The interface is plainer and support is thinner — but if price is your only criterion, it's the strongest do-it-yourself deal in the market today.

turbotaxAlternative.taxlyBody: Taxly's case is one sentence: the price you see when you start is the price you pay when you file. Simple W-2 returns free (federal plus one state), $39 for Deluxe, $69 for self-employed — every plan includes your first state, extra states are $29 each — locked at the start, no mid-flow upgrade ambush. We're pre-launch (opening for the 2027 season), so today the honest offer is a waitlist spot, not a checkout.
turbotaxAlternative.taxlyLink: Read the full Taxly vs TurboTax comparison
turbotaxAlternative.stayHeading: When you should just stay with TurboTax
stayItems: Your return is genuinely complex — multi-state, K-1s, rare credits — and you want the deepest software coverage available
You've used it for years, your history imports cleanly, and the checkout price doesn't actually bother you
You want the biggest brand's audit-support apparatus behind your return
turbotaxAlternative.ctaHeading: Want the price you saw to be the price you pay?
turbotaxAlternative.ctaBody: Join the waitlist and we'll email you when Taxly opens for the season.

faq.q: What is the best free alternative to TurboTax?
faq.a: If you qualify, IRS Direct File — the government's own tool, free for federal and participating-state returns. If your income is under the yearly cap, IRS Free File gives you brand-name software free. FreeTaxUSA covers most other do-it-yourself situations with free federal filing and a small state fee.

faq.q: Why is TurboTax so expensive at checkout?
faq.a: The advertised price and the checkout price often differ: the flow prompts upgrades when your situation needs another form, and state returns are charged separately. That pricing model is legal, common — and exactly what flat, price-locked tiers exist to avoid.

faq.q: Is Taxly available now?
faq.a: Not yet — Taxly is pre-launch and opens for the 2027 filing season. The prices on this page are the planned, price-locked tiers; the waitlist gets first access.

faq.q: Is switching tax software hard?
faq.a: Less than you'd think. Your prior-year return (a PDF from any major software) carries the numbers a new tool needs. The IRS also keeps wage and income transcripts you can pull for free at irs.gov if you're missing documents.

freeTaxFiling.heading: Actually free tax filing — every real option
freeTaxFiling.intro: 'Free' is the most abused word in tax software — regulators went after the biggest brand over it. Here is every way to file that is genuinely free, who each one fits, and what 'free' usually hides. We sell tax filing, so read this as a competitor showing you the free exits first.

options.name: IRS Direct File
options.body: The government's own tool. No income cap, no upsell — but a strict situation gate: W-2 income, standard deduction, common credits, participating states only. If you fit, it's the cleanest free option in America.

options.name: IRS Free File
options.body: Commercial software free through an IRS partnership when your income is under the program's yearly cap (verify the current figure on irs.gov). Enter through irs.gov — not through the brands' own sites, where the 'free' path has a way of ending at a paywall.

options.name: VITA and AARP Tax-Aide
options.body: IRS-certified volunteers prepare your return face to face, free — aimed at moderate incomes, older filers, disabilities, and limited English. Income limits and site availability vary; find a location through the locator on irs.gov.

options.name: Taxly's free tier
options.body: When we launch for the 2027 season: simple W-2 returns free — federal plus one state — and the price for everything else shown before you start and locked. We're pre-launch today — the waitlist is the only thing on sale.

hidesItems: State returns — the classic: federal is free, then each state costs $30–$60 at checkout
Form-triggered upgrades — a 1099, HSA, or itemized deduction quietly moves you to a paid tier
Paying from your refund — a convenience that typically adds its own processing fee
'Free guidance' that turns into a paid review before you can file
freeTaxFiling.honestyHeading: Our own fine print, stated plainly
freeTaxFiling.honestyBody: Taxly's free tier covers simple W-2 returns — federal plus one state included. Deluxe is $39 and self-employment is $69, each including your first state return; additional states are $29 each — not free, and we'd rather say so here than surprise you at checkout. Every price is locked when you start.
freeTaxFiling.ctaHeading: Want filing without the fine print?
freeTaxFiling.ctaBody: Join the waitlist — first access when Taxly opens for the season.

faq.q: Is there a truly free way to file taxes?
faq.a: Yes, several: IRS Direct File (if your situation qualifies), IRS Free File (if your income is under the cap), VITA/AARP volunteer preparation (moderate incomes, seniors), and free tiers like Taxly's for simple W-2 returns. Each has a gate — free always comes with a 'who fits' condition.

faq.q: Why did 'free' tax filing become controversial?
faq.a: Because 'free' ads routinely led to paid checkouts. US regulators ruled against the biggest brand's advertising over exactly this. The safe habit: enter free programs through irs.gov, and treat any mid-flow upgrade prompt as a price tag.

faq.q: Is state filing ever free?
faq.a: Sometimes: IRS Direct File includes participating states, some Free File offers include state returns, and a few states run their own free e-file portals. With commercial software, assume state costs extra until you see otherwise in writing.

faq.q: What does Taxly charge?
faq.a: Planned pricing, locked at the start: simple W-2 returns free (federal plus one state), $39 Deluxe, $69 self-employed, $29 per additional state. Taxly is pre-launch and opens for the 2027 season — join the waitlist for first access.

faq.q: If free options exist, why pay Taxly at all?
faq.a: If a free gate fits your whole situation, use it — that’s what this page is for. You pay Taxly when your situation outgrows the gates (1099 income, an HSA, itemizing, a non-participating state) and you’d rather see a locked 9 or 9 up front than hit a surprise paywall mid-flow — with your first state included and three written guarantees standing behind the return.

why.heading: The honest case for paying us
why.intro: If a free option above covers your whole situation, use it — we mean that. Here is where we earn the fee instead:

items.title: Your situation outgrew the free gates
items.text: A 1099 side gig, an HSA, itemizing, or a state that doesn’t participate pushes you out of every free gate above. On ambush software that’s the moment a paywall appears mid-flow. With Taxly you saw 9 or 9 on the first screen — and it was locked.

items.title: Your first state return is included
items.text: Every Taxly tier — including Free — covers federal plus your first state. The classic “free federal, 0 state at checkout” move is the exact trick this page exists to warn you about, so we designed it out.

items.title: Someone stands behind the return
items.text: Three written guarantees: maximum refund, accuracy — a penalty caused by our math is ours to pay — and audit guidance when the IRS writes to you. File with the free government tool and you’re on your own the day after acceptance.

items.title: Plain English, start to finish
items.text: A guided interview asks questions and fills the 1040 behind the scenes — no raw form fields, no jargon, and your prior return imports from a TurboTax or H&R Block PDF instead of being retyped.
